title: Word search
description: Check if word exists in grid

examples.explanation: 1. Start at (0,0) with 'A' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 2. From (0,0): try down to (1,0) 'S' (no match), right to (0,1) 'B' (matches word[1]). Mark (0,1). 3. From (0,1): try down to (1,1) 'F' (no match), right to (0,2) 'C' (matches word[2]). Mark (0,2). 4. From (0,2): try down to (1,2) 'C' (matches word[3]). Mark (1,2). 5. From (1,2): try down to (2,2) 'E' (matches word[4]). Mark (2,2). 6. From (2,2): try left to (2,1) 'D' (matches word[5]). All characters matched, return true. Backtrack unmarks all visited cells.

1. Start at (1,0) 'S' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 2. From (1,0): try up to (0,0) 'A' (no match), down to (2,0) 'A' (no match), right to (1,1) 'F' (no match). Backtrack. 3. Start at (1,3) 'S' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 4. From (1,3): try up to (0,3) 'E' (matches word[1]). Mark (0,3). 5. From (0,3): try down to (1,3) already visited, left to (0,2) 'C' (no match). Backtrack to (1,3). 6. From (1,3): try down to (2,3) 'E' (matches word[1]). Mark (2,3). 7. From (2,3): try up to (1,3) already visited, left to (2,2) 'E' (matches word[2]). All characters matched, return true.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from every cell in the grid, exploring all possible paths to match the word, backtracking when mismatches occur. This is a straightforward recursive approach that exhaustively checks all directions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if a word exists in a 2D grid by exploring all possible paths from every starting cell.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete word search problem solved using brute force DFS. The intuition is that we need to check every possible path in the grid that could form the target word. Since we don't know where the word might start, we must try every cell as a potential starting point. For each starting cell, we perform a depth-first search exploring all four directions (up, down, left, right) recursively, building the path character by character. We use backtracking to mark visited cells temporarily to avoid reusing the same cell in a single path, then unmark them when backtracking. The algorithm works because it exhaustively explores all possible paths of length L (the word length) starting from every cell. The time complexity is O(m*n*4^L) because we have m*n starting points and at each step we explore up to 4 directions for L steps. Space complexity is O(L) for the recursion stack and visited tracking. This connects to L1 by using the DFS helper function as the core recursive exploration mechanism. Common mistakes include: not marking visited cells (causing infinite loops), not unmarking visited cells during backtracking (preventing exploration of alternative paths), and not checking bounds before accessing grid cells. The real code pattern involves: 1) iterating through all cells as starting points, 2) calling DFS helper, 3) implementing DFS with base cases (word found, out of bounds, character mismatch), 4) marking visited, 5) recursing in 4 directions, 6) unmarking visited during backtrack.

examples.trace: 1. Start at (0,0) with 'A' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 2. From (0,0): try down to (1,0) 'S' (no match), right to (0,1) 'B' (matches word[1]). Mark (0,1). 3. From (0,1): try down to (1,1) 'F' (no match), right to (0,2) 'C' (matches word[2]). Mark (0,2). 4. From (0,2): try down to (1,2) 'C' (matches word[3]). Mark (1,2). 5. From (1,2): try down to (2,2) 'E' (matches word[4]). Mark (2,2). 6. From (2,2): try left to (2,1) 'D' (matches word[5]). All characters matched, return true. Backtrack unmarks all visited cells.

1. Start at (1,0) 'S' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 2. From (1,0): try up to (0,0) 'A' (no match), down to (2,0) 'A' (no match), right to (1,1) 'F' (no match). Backtrack. 3. Start at (1,3) 'S' (matches word[0]). Mark visited. 4. From (1,3): try up to (0,3) 'E' (matches word[1]). Mark (0,3). 5. From (0,3): try down to (1,3) already visited, left to (0,2) 'C' (no match). Backtrack to (1,3). 6. From (1,3): try down to (2,3) 'E' (matches word[1]). Mark (2,3). 7. From (2,3): try up to (1,3) already visited, left to (2,2) 'E' (matches word[2]). All characters matched, return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper Function
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores from a specific cell to match remaining characters of the word.
ladders.explanation: The DFS helper function is the core recursive component that explores paths from a given starting position. The intuition is that once we've matched some prefix of the word at a particular cell, we need to check if we can extend this path to match the remaining characters. The function takes the current grid position (i,j) and the current index in the word we're trying to match. Base cases: 1) If we've matched all characters (index == word length), return true (success). 2) If position is out of bounds, return false. 3) If current cell doesn't match word[index], return false. 4) If cell is already visited in current path, return false. If we pass all base cases, we mark the cell as visited, then recursively explore all four directions (up, down, left, right). If any direction returns true, we propagate true upward. Before returning, we unmark the cell (backtracking) to allow it to be used in other paths. Time complexity for this function alone is O(4^L) in worst case as it explores up to 4 branches at each depth for L levels. Space complexity is O(L) for recursion stack and visited tracking. This connects to L0 as the function called from each starting cell in the main loop. Common mistakes: forgetting to check bounds before accessing grid, not handling the 'all characters matched' base case first, improper backtracking. Real code pattern: def dfs(i, j, index): if index == len(word): return True; if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n: return False; if grid[i][j] != word[index]: return False; if visited[i][j]: return False; visited[i][j] = True; for each direction (di,dj) in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: if dfs(i+di, j+dj, index+1): return True; visited[i][j] = False; return False.

examples.input: Starting at (0,0) in grid [['A','B'],['C','D']] with word 'AB', index=0
examples.trace: 1. dfs(0,0,0): grid[0][0]='A' matches word[0]='A', not visited. Mark visited. 2. Explore directions: down to (1,0) 'C' doesn't match 'B', right to (0,1) 'B' matches word[1]. Call dfs(0,1,1). 3. dfs(0,1,1): index=1 == word length=2, return true. 4. Propagate true back to original call, return true.

examples.input: Starting at (0,0) in grid [['A','A'],['A','A']] with word 'AAA', index=0
examples.trace: 1. dfs(0,0,0): matches 'A', mark visited. 2. Explore: down to (1,0) 'A' matches word[1]. Call dfs(1,0,1). 3. dfs(1,0,1): matches 'A', mark visited. 4. Explore: right to (1,1) 'A' matches word[2]. Call dfs(1,1,2). 5. dfs(1,1,2): index=2 == word length=3, return true. 6. Propagate true back through chain, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Technique of marking/unmarking cells during DFS to avoid reusing cells in a single path.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking with visited tracking is essential for exploring all possible paths without getting stuck in cycles. The intuition is that when building a path to match a word, we cannot use the same cell twice in that path (though different paths can reuse cells). We need to temporarily mark a cell as 'visited' when we include it in our current path, then unmark it when we backtrack to explore alternative paths. This creates a stateful exploration where each recursive call maintains which cells are currently in use. The algorithm: 1) Before exploring from a cell, mark it as visited (e.g., set visited[i][j] = True or modify grid[i][j] to a special marker). 2) Recursively explore neighbors. 3) After exploring all neighbors, unmark the cell (visited[i][j] = False or restore original character). This ensures that when we return to a previous level in the recursion, the cell becomes available for other path explorations. Time complexity for this pattern is inherent in the DFS exploration. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the visited array if using separate storage, or O(1) extra if modifying grid in-place (though that has other drawbacks). This connects to L1 as the mechanism that enables the DFS helper to work correctly. Common mistakes: forgetting to unmark (causing cells to remain permanently unavailable), using the wrong data structure for visited tracking, not restoring original grid values if modifying in-place. Real code pattern: visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; in dfs: visited[i][j] = True; recurse; visited[i][j] = False. Alternative: temp = grid[i][j]; grid[i][j] = '#'; recurse; grid[i][j] = temp.

examples.input: Grid 2x2 all 'A's, starting at (0,0), exploring path for 'AAA'
examples.trace: 1. Mark (0,0) visited. 2. Go down to (1,0), mark visited. 3. Go right to (1,1), mark visited. Path complete for 'AAA'. 4. Backtrack: unmark (1,1). 5. From (1,0): try right to (1,1) now available again. 6. Mark (1,1) visited. Alternative path explored. Without unmarking, (1,1) would remain unavailable after first use.

1. For 'AB': mark (0,0), go right to (0,1), mark it, found 'AB'. Unmark (0,1), unmark (0,0). 2. For 'AC': mark (0,0), go down to (1,0), mark it, found 'AC'. The unmarking after 'AB' allows (0,0) to be reused as start for 'AC' path.

testCases.input: Grid 2x2 with all different letters, start at (0,0), explore 2-step paths
testCases.expected: Can explore all 4 possible 2-step paths from (0,0)

testCases.input: Grid 1x3 with repeating letters, start at (0,0), explore 3-step path
testCases.expected: Can find path using all cells without getting stuck

testCases.input: Grid [['A','A'],['A','A']], test backtracking after failed path
testCases.expected: Cells become available again for new paths

testCases.input: Grid with visited array initially all False

testCases.expected: Cells can be part of multiple paths sequentially

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with Boundary Checking
ladders.desc: Safely navigating a 2D grid while checking array bounds at each step.
ladders.explanation: Grid traversal with boundary checking is fundamental for any algorithm working with 2D arrays. The intuition is that when moving in a grid (up, down, left, right), we must ensure we don't access memory outside the grid boundaries, which would cause errors. The grid has dimensions m rows and n columns, with indices typically ranging from 0 to m-1 and 0 to n-1. Before accessing any cell (i,j), we check: 0 <= i < m and 0 <= j < n. This prevents index out of bounds errors. When exploring neighbors, we generate candidate positions by adding direction vectors: up: (i-1, j), down: (i+1, j), left: (i, j-1), right: (i, j+1). Each candidate must pass the boundary check before being processed. The algorithm: 1) Define grid dimensions m and n. 2) For a given position (i,j), generate neighbor positions using direction vectors. 3) For each neighbor, if 0 <= new_i < m and 0 <= new_j < n, it's valid; otherwise, skip it. Time complexity for checking bounds is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as boundary checking is performed before marking visited or comparing characters in DFS. Common mistakes: forgetting to check bounds, using wrong comparison operators (e.g., i <= m instead of i < m), not handling edge cases like empty grids. Real code pattern: directions = [(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni, nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n: process cell (ni,nj).

examples.trace: 1. Start at (0,0). 2. Check up: (-1,0) -> i=-1 < 0, invalid. 3. Check down: (1,0) -> i=1 (0<=1<3), j=0 (0<=0<3), valid. 4. Check left: (0,-1) -> j=-1 < 0, invalid. 5. Check right: (0,1) -> i=0, j=1 (0<=1<3), valid. Total valid neighbors: 2.

1. Start at (1,1). 2. Check up: (0,1) -> i=0 (0<=0<2), j=1 (0<=1<2), valid. 3. Check down: (2,1) -> i=2 >=2, invalid. 4. Check left: (1,0) -> i=1, j=0 (0<=0<2), valid. 5. Check right: (1,2) -> j=2 >=2, invalid. Total valid neighbors: 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison in 2D Array
ladders.desc: Accessing and comparing characters at specific positions in a 2D grid.
ladders.explanation: Character comparison in a 2D array is the fundamental operation of checking if a grid cell contains a specific character. The intuition is simple: we have a 2D array (list of lists in Python) where each element is a character, and we need to compare it with a target character from our word. We access the cell using row and column indices: grid[i][j] gives the character at row i, column j. We then compare this with word[k] (the k-th character of our target word) using equality operator (==). This operation is performed repeatedly during DFS to verify if our current path matches the word prefix. The algorithm: 1) Ensure indices i and j are within bounds (from L3). 2) Access grid[i][j]. 3) Compare with word[k]. 4) If equal, continue; otherwise, path fails at this point. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's performed after boundary checking in the DFS flow. Common mistakes: confusing row/column order, using wrong index for word, not handling case sensitivity if required. Real code pattern: if grid[i][j] == word[index]: proceed; else: return False.

examples.trace: 1. Access grid[0][1] = 'B'. 2. Compare 'B' == 'B'. 3. Result: true.
1. Access grid[1][2] = 'R'. 2. Compare 'R' == 'Q'. 3. Result: false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Elements in 2D Lists
ladders.desc: Understanding how to store and retrieve data from two-dimensional arrays/lists.
ladders.explanation: Accessing elements in 2D lists is the most basic prerequisite for grid-based problems. A 2D list (or array) is essentially a list of lists, where each inner list represents a row. For example, grid = [['A','B','C'], ['D','E','F']] has 2 rows and 3 columns. To access an element, we use two indices: grid[row][column]. The first index selects the row (0-based), the second selects the column within that row. It's crucial to understand that grid[i] gives the i-th row (a list), and grid[i][j] gives the j-th element in that row. The grid has dimensions: number of rows = len(grid), number of columns = len(grid[0]) assuming all rows have same length. This foundation is necessary because all grid algorithms start with accessing cells. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity for storage is O(m*n). This connects to L4 as it enables the character comparison operation. Common beginner mistakes: confusing row/column order (thinking grid[column][row]), using 1-based indexing, not understanding nested list structure. Real code pattern: element = grid[i][j] to get value; grid[i][j] = value to set value.

examples.trace: 1. grid is a list with 2 elements: grid[0] = ['A','B'], grid[1] = ['C','D']. 2. grid[0][1] means: take grid[0] (first row) which is ['A','B'], then take element at index 1 of that list, which is 'B'.
1. grid[1] = ['P','Q','R'] (second row). 2. grid[1][2] = element at index 2 of ['P','Q','R'], which is 'R' (indices: 0='P', 1='Q', 2='R').

approaches.name: Backtracking with Pruning O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Use DFS with backtracking but incorporate early termination by checking if the remaining word length exceeds available cells or using a visited set to avoid cycles. This reduces unnecessary explorations compared to brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Backtracking and Pruning
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if a word exists in a 2D grid using DFS backtracking with early termination optimizations.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the complete word search problem using backtracking with pruning. The intuition is to treat the grid as a graph where each cell is a node connected to its four neighbors (up, down, left, right). We perform DFS from every starting cell that matches the first character of the word, exploring all possible paths while backtracking when we hit dead ends. The key optimization is pruning: we stop exploring a path if the remaining word length exceeds the maximum possible path length from the current position (considering grid boundaries), and we use a visited set to avoid cycles. This reduces unnecessary explorations compared to brute force. The algorithm: 1) For each cell in the grid, if it matches the first character of the word, call DFS. 2) DFS function takes current position, visited set, and word index. 3) Base cases: if word index equals word length (found), return true; if out of bounds or cell doesn't match current character or cell visited, return false. 4) Mark cell as visited. 5) Recursively explore four directions. 6) If any direction returns true, return true. 7) Backtrack: unmark cell. 8) Return false if all directions fail. Time complexity is O(m*n*4^L) where m,n are grid dimensions and L is word length (worst-case exploring all 4^L paths from each cell). Space complexity is O(L) for recursion stack and visited set. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS backtracking subroutine as the core recursive engine. Common mistakes: forgetting to backtrack (unmark visited cells), not checking bounds properly, missing the optimization to start only from matching first characters. Real code pattern: def exist(grid, word): m,n=len(grid),len(grid[0]); def dfs(i,j,idx,visited): if idx==len(word): return True; if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or (i,j) in visited or grid[i][j]!=word[idx]: return False; visited.add((i,j)); for di,dj in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: if dfs(i+di,j+dj,idx+1,visited): return True; visited.remove((i,j)); return False; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): if grid[i][j]==word[0] and dfs(i,j,0,set()): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start: grid 3x4, word 'ABCCED' length 6. Check all cells for 'A': (0,0)='A' matches. DFS from (0,0): idx=0, visited={(0,0)}. Explore neighbors: (1,0)='S'≠'B' skip; (0,1)='B' matches 'B', idx=1, visited={(0,0),(0,1)}. From (0,1): (1,1)='F'≠'C' skip; (0,2)='C' matches 'C', idx=2, visited={(0,0),(0,1),(0,2)}. From (0,2): (1,2)='C' matches 'C', idx=3, visited add (1,2). From (1,2): (2,2)='E' matches 'E', idx=4, visited add (2,2). From (2,2): (2,1)='D' matches 'D', idx=5, visited add (2,1). From (2,1): neighbors: (1,1)='F'≠'E' skip; (2,0)='A'≠'E' skip; (2,2) visited skip; (3,1) out of bounds. Backtrack to (2,2): try other neighbors: (1,2) visited skip; (2,3)='E' matches 'E', idx=6, visited add (2,3). idx=6 equals word length, return true. Propagation returns true to top. Output true.

Start: word 'SEE' length 3. Check cells for 'S': (1,0)='S' matches. DFS from (1,0): idx=0, visited={(1,0)}. Explore: (0,0)='A'≠'E' skip; (2,0)='A'≠'E' skip; (1,1)='F'≠'E' skip; (1,-1) out of bounds. All fail, backtrack, visited remove (1,0). Next 'S' at (1,3)='S'. DFS from (1,3): idx=0, visited={(1,3)}. Explore: (0,3)='E' matches 'E', idx=1, visited add (0,3). From (0,3): neighbors: (-1,3) out; (1,3) visited; (0,2)='C'≠'E' skip; (0,4) out. All fail, backtrack to (1,3). Try other neighbors: (2,3)='E' matches 'E', idx=1, visited add (2,3). From (2,3): neighbors: (1,3) visited; (3,3) out; (2,2)='E' matches 'E', idx=2, visited add (2,2). idx=2, word[2]='E' matches, continue: idx=3 equals length, return true. Output true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Backtracking for Path Exploration
ladders.desc: Implement DFS with backtracking to explore all possible paths from a starting cell while matching characters.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that explores paths in the grid. The intuition is to simulate walking through the grid, matching characters one by one, and backtracking when we hit a mismatch or dead end. It's a depth-first search because we go as deep as possible along one path before trying alternatives. Backtracking is essential to undo visited marks so other paths can reuse cells. The algorithm: 1) Input: current grid cell (i,j), current index in word (idx), visited set. 2) Base cases: if idx equals word length, return true (found); if out of bounds or cell visited or cell doesn't match word[idx], return false. 3) Mark cell as visited. 4) Recursively call function for four neighbors (up, down, left, right) with idx+1. 5) If any neighbor returns true, return true. 6) Otherwise, unmark cell (backtrack) and return false. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(4^L) in worst case (exploring all paths of length L). Space complexity is O(L) for recursion depth and visited set. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main loop for each starting cell. Connection to L2: This implements the backtracking logic with pruning via base cases. Common mistakes: not using a visited set (leads to cycles), forgetting to backtrack (causes cells to remain marked), incorrect base case order. Real code pattern: def dfs(i,j,idx,visited): if idx==len(word): return True; if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or (i,j) in visited or grid[i][j]!=word[idx]: return False; visited.add((i,j)); for di,dj in directions: if dfs(i+di,j+dj,idx+1,visited): return True; visited.remove((i,j)); return False.

examples.trace: DFS call: i=0,j=0,idx=0,visited={}. Check: idx=0≠2, in bounds, not visited, grid[0][0]='A' matches word[0]='A'. Mark visited: visited={(0,0)}. Explore neighbors: (1,0)='C'≠'B' skip; (0,1)='B' matches 'B', call dfs(0,1,1,{(0,0)}). In dfs(0,1): idx=1≠2, in bounds, not visited, grid[0][1]='B' matches word[1]='B'. Mark: visited={(0,0),(0,1)}. Explore neighbors: (1,1)='D'≠word[2] but idx=2 would be out of range, so base case: idx=2 equals word length 2, return true. Propagate true back. Unmark (0,1) and (0,0) during returns. Output true.

DFS from (0,0): idx=0≠3, in bounds, not visited, grid[0][0]='X' matches 'X'. Mark visited={(0,0)}. Explore: (1,0)='Z'≠'Y' skip; (0,1)='Y' matches 'Y', call dfs(0,1,1,{(0,0)}). In dfs(0,1): idx=1≠3, in bounds, not visited, grid[0][1]='Y' matches 'Y'. Mark visited={(0,0),(0,1)}. Explore neighbors: (1,1)='W'≠'Z' skip; (-1,1) out; (0,2) out; (0,0) visited. All fail, backtrack: remove (0,1), return false to (0,0). From (0,0), try other neighbors: (-1,0) out; (0,-1) out. All fail, backtrack: remove (0,0), return false. Output false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Pruning for Early Termination
ladders.desc: Implement the backtracking mechanism with checks to stop exploring impossible paths early.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the backtracking technique enhanced with pruning to avoid unnecessary explorations. The intuition is that in DFS, we can terminate a path as soon as we know it cannot lead to a solution, saving computation. Pruning here includes: checking if the current cell matches the required character, ensuring we don't revisit cells (cycle prevention), and verifying bounds. The core logic is: at each step, validate conditions before recursing; if invalid, return immediately. This is implemented via base cases in the recursive function. The algorithm: 1) Check if current position is valid (within bounds, not visited, character matches). 2) If invalid, return false (prune). 3) If valid and we've matched all characters, return true. 4) Otherwise, mark as visited and recurse to neighbors. 5) After exploring all neighbors, unmark (backtrack). Time complexity benefits from pruning by reducing the number of recursive calls. Space complexity is O(L) for recursion and visited set. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the DFS subroutine's base cases. Connection to L3: Pruning relies on basic comparisons and set operations. Common mistakes: pruning too aggressively (e.g., stopping when word length exceeds grid size globally, which is incorrect for specific paths), not pruning at all (leads to brute force). Real code pattern: if idx==len(word): return True; if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or (i,j) in visited or grid[i][j]!=word[idx]: return False; visited.add((i,j)); for each neighbor: if dfs(neighbor, idx+1, visited): return True; visited.remove((i,j)); return False.

examples.input: Check pruning at cell (0,0) with grid[0][0]='A', word[0]='B', idx=0, visited empty
examples.trace: Step: idx=0, word length >0. Check conditions: in bounds yes, not visited yes, but grid[0][0]='A' != word[0]='B'. Prune immediately: return false without recursing. No further exploration from this cell.

examples.input: Check pruning for visited cell: at (1,1) with visited={(1,1)}, grid[1][1]='X', word[0]='X', idx=0
examples.trace: Step: idx=0. Check: in bounds yes, but (1,1) is in visited set. Prune: return false immediately. Avoids cycle where we might loop back to same cell.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with Bounds Checking and Neighbor Generation
ladders.desc: Implement functions to traverse a 2D grid, check boundaries, and generate valid neighbor coordinates.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves navigating a 2D grid, which is fundamental to the word search problem. The intuition is that the grid can be represented as a matrix with rows and columns; we need to move between cells while staying within bounds. Key operations: 1) Check if a coordinate (i,j) is within grid dimensions (0<=i<m, 0<=j<n). 2) Generate the four adjacent neighbors: up (i-1,j), down (i+1,j), left (i,j-1), right (i,j+1). 3) Filter neighbors to only those within bounds. This building block ensures we don't access invalid memory and provides the possible moves for DFS. The algorithm: define directions = [(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)]; for each (di,dj) in directions, new_i = i+di, new_j = j+dj; if 0<=new_i<m and 0<=new_j<n, then (new_i,new_j) is a valid neighbor. Time complexity for generating neighbors is O(1) per cell (constant 4 checks). Space complexity is O(1) for storing directions. Connection to L2: This provides the mechanism to explore next cells in backtracking. Connection to L4: Relies on basic array indexing and comparisons. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds checks, incorrect direction vectors, forgetting to handle negative indices. Real code pattern: directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n: process neighbor.

examples.trace: Start at (1,1). Directions: (1,0) -> (2,1): 0<=2<3 and 0<=1<3: valid. (-1,0) -> (0,1): valid. (0,1) -> (1,2): valid. (0,-1) -> (1,0): valid. All four are valid since (1,1) is interior.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and Set Operations for Visited Cells
ladders.desc: Implement basic operations to compare grid characters with word characters and manage a set of visited coordinates.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key tasks: 1) Comparing characters: check if the character at grid[i][j] equals the character at word[idx]. This is a simple equality check but crucial for matching the word. 2) Managing visited cells: use a set to store coordinates (i,j) that have been used in the current path to prevent revisiting. Operations include adding a coordinate to the set when visiting, checking if a coordinate is in the set to avoid cycles, and removing it when backtracking. The intuition is that sets provide O(1) average-time membership tests, making them efficient for this purpose. The algorithm: for comparison: if grid[i][j] == word[idx]: proceed, else prune. For visited set: visited.add((i,j)) to mark, (i,j) in visited to check, visited.remove((i,j)) to unmark. Time complexity: O(1) per operation. Space complexity: O(L) for the set storing up to L coordinates. Connection to L3: These operations are used during grid traversal to validate moves. Connection to L5: Relies on basic data structures like strings and sets. Common mistakes: using a list for visited (slow membership checks), not using tuples for coordinates (lists are unhashable), incorrect comparison indices. Real code pattern: if grid[i][j] != word[idx]: return False; visited.add((i,j)); if (i,j) in visited: return False; visited.remove((i,j)).

examples.trace: grid[0][0] = 'A', word[0] = 'A'. Compare: 'A' == 'A', so match is true. Proceed.

examples.input: visited set operations: start empty, add (1,2), check (1,2), remove (1,2)
examples.trace: Initial visited = set() = {}. Add (1,2): visited.add((1,2)) -> visited = {(1,2)}. Check (1,2) in visited: (1,2) is in set, returns True. Remove (1,2): visited.remove((1,2)) -> visited = {}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Array Indexing and Basic Recursion
ladders.desc: Understand how to access elements in a 2D array and write a simple recursive function.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. For 2D array indexing: a 2D array (or list of lists in Python) is a grid where each element is accessed using two indices: grid[i][j] gives the element at row i, column j. Rows are typically indexed from 0 to m-1, columns from 0 to n-1. The intuition is like coordinates on a map. For basic recursion: a recursive function calls itself with modified parameters until a base case is reached. It has two parts: base case(s) that stop recursion, and recursive case(s) that call the function again. The intuition is solving a problem by breaking it into smaller similar subproblems. Example: factorial(n) returns n * factorial(n-1) with base case n=0. In word search, recursion explores paths by calling itself for neighbor cells. Time complexity depends on recursion depth; space complexity includes call stack. Connection to L4: Recursion is used to implement DFS, and array indexing accesses grid characters. Common mistakes: infinite recursion due to missing base case, incorrect indices leading to out-of-bounds errors. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(param): if base_case: return value; else: return recursive_func(modified_param). For 2D array: element = grid[row][col].

examples.trace: grid is a list of two lists: row 0 is ['a','b'], row 1 is ['c','d']. grid[0] gives first row ['a','b']. grid[0][1] gives element at index 1 of that row: 'b'.

examples.input: Recursive function to compute sum of first n natural numbers: sum(n)=n+sum(n-1), base case sum(0)=0, call sum(3)
examples.trace: Call sum(3): n=3 not 0, so return 3 + sum(2). Call sum(2): return 2 + sum(1). Call sum(1): return 1 + sum(0). Call sum(0): base case, return 0. Back propagate: sum(1)=1+0=1; sum(2)=2+1=3; sum(3)=3+3=6.

testCases.input: recursive factorial: fact(0)=1, fact(n)=n*fact(n-1), call fact(5)
recursive fibonacci: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1, fib(n)=fib(n-1)+fib(n-2), call fib(4)
recursive sum: sum(0)=0, sum(n)=n+sum(n-1), call sum(10)
recursive power: pow(x,0)=1, pow(x,n)=x*pow(x,n-1), call pow(2,3)

approaches.name: Trie-Optimized Backtracking O(m*n*4^L)
approaches.summary: Build a trie from the word list (if multiple words) or use a prefix check to stop DFS when the current path cannot form a valid prefix. This avoids exploring paths that won't lead to a solution.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(L + T) where T is trie size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Search with Trie-Optimized Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if a word exists in a grid using backtracking with trie-based prefix pruning.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the classic word search problem where we need to determine if a given word exists in a 2D character grid by forming a path of adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically). The key optimization uses a trie data structure to prune backtracking paths early when the current path cannot form a valid prefix of the target word. The intuition is that without prefix checking, we would explore all 4^L possible paths from each cell (where L is word length), but with a trie, we stop as soon as the current character sequence doesn't match any prefix of the target word. The algorithm works by first building a trie from the target word (or simply tracking prefixes if only one word). Then we perform DFS/backtracking from each cell in the grid. At each step, we check if the current cell's character matches the next character needed in the word via trie traversal. If not, we backtrack. If we reach the end of the word in the trie (a complete match), we return true. We mark visited cells to avoid reusing them in the same path. Time complexity is O(m*n*4^L) in worst case but significantly better in practice due to pruning. Space complexity is O(L + T) where T is trie size (O(L) for single word). Connection to L1: This integrates the backtracking DFS (L1) with trie-based prefix checking. Common mistakes include not marking/unmarking visited cells correctly, not handling edge cases (empty grid, empty word), and incorrect trie traversal. Real code pattern: Build trie, iterate through grid cells, call DFS with current cell and trie node, check boundaries/visited, check character match via trie, mark visited, recurse to neighbors, unmark visited, return result.

examples.trace: 1. Build trie for 'ABCCED': root->A->B->C->C->E->D (end). 2. Start from (0,0): 'A' matches trie root->A, mark visited, move to node A. 3. From (0,0) explore neighbors: (0,1) 'B' matches A->B, mark visited, move to node B. 4. From (0,1): (0,2) 'C' matches B->C, mark visited, move to node C. 5. From (0,2): (1,2) 'C' matches C->C, mark visited, move to node C (second C). 6. From (1,2): (1,3) 'E' matches C->E, mark visited, move to node E. 7. From (1,3): (2,3) 'E' doesn't match E->D, backtrack. Try (0,3) 'E' from (1,2): matches C->E, mark visited, move to node E. 8. From (0,3): (0,2) visited, (1,3) visited, no valid neighbors, backtrack. From (1,2): (2,2) 'E' matches C->E, mark visited, move to node E. 9. From (2,2): (2,3) 'E' doesn't match E->D, (2,1) 'D' matches E->D, mark visited, move to node D (end node). 10. Reached end of word at (2,1), return true.

1. Build trie for 'SEE': root->S->E->E (end). 2. Start from (1,0): 'S' matches root->S, mark visited, move to node S. 3. From (1,0): (0,0) 'A' doesn't match S->E, (1,1) 'F' doesn't match, (2,0) 'A' doesn't match, backtrack. 4. Start from (1,3): 'S' matches root->S, mark visited, move to node S. 5. From (1,3): (0,3) 'E' matches S->E, mark visited, move to node E. 6. From (0,3): (0,2) 'C' doesn't match E->E, (1,3) visited, backtrack to (1,3). 7. From (1,3): (2,3) 'E' matches S->E, mark visited, move to node E. 8. From (2,3): (2,2) 'E' matches E->E, mark visited, move to node E (end). 9. Reached end of word at (2,2), return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking DFS with Trie Node
ladders.desc: DFS function that explores grid paths while traversing a trie to check prefix validity.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that performs depth-first search with backtracking through the grid, using a trie node to track progress in the word. The intuition is that we need to explore all possible paths from a starting cell, but we can prune paths early using the trie: if the current character doesn't match the next expected character in the trie, we stop exploring that branch. The algorithm takes current grid coordinates (i,j), current trie node, and visited matrix. Base cases: if trie node marks end of word, return true; if out of bounds or cell visited, return false; if grid[i][j] doesn't match current trie node's children, return false. Recursive case: mark cell visited, get child trie node for grid[i][j], recursively call DFS on all four neighbors (up, down, left, right) with the child node, unmark visited, return true if any neighbor returns true. Time complexity for this function alone is O(4^L) in worst case but pruned by trie. Space complexity is O(L) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called from each cell in the main loop. Connection to L2: This implements the backtracking pattern with trie integration. Common mistakes: forgetting to unmark visited cells (causing paths to be blocked), not checking trie node existence before accessing children, incorrect base case order. Real code pattern: function dfs(i, j, node, visited): if node.is_end: return true; if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or visited[i][j]: return false; char = grid[i][j]; if char not in node.children: return false; visited[i][j]=true; next_node=node.children[char]; result = dfs(i+1,j,next_node,visited) or dfs(i-1,j,next_node,visited) or dfs(i,j+1,next_node,visited) or dfs(i,j-1,next_node,visited); visited[i][j]=false; return result.

examples.input: grid = [['A','B'],['C','D']], start=(0,0), word='AB', trie built for 'AB', visited all false
examples.trace: 1. Call dfs(0,0, root_node, visited). root_node has child 'A'. 2. Check: node not end, (0,0) in bounds, not visited, grid[0][0]='A' in node.children. 3. Mark visited[0][0]=true, get next_node for 'A'. 4. Recurse to neighbor (0,1): dfs(0,1, node_A, visited). node_A has child 'B'. 5. Check: node_A not end, (0,1) in bounds, not visited, grid[0][1]='B' in node_A.children. 6. Mark visited[0][1]=true, get next_node for 'B' (end node). 7. Recurse to neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2) out of bounds, (1,1) 'D' not in node_B.children, (-1,1) out of bounds. All return false. 8. At (0,1): node_B.is_end is true, return true. 9. Back to (0,0): result=true, unmark visited[0][0]=false, return true.

examples.input: grid = [['A','B'],['C','D']], start=(0,0), word='AC', trie built for 'AC', visited all false
examples.trace: 1. Call dfs(0,0, root_node, visited). root_node has child 'A'. 2. Check: node not end, (0,0) in bounds, not visited, grid[0][0]='A' in node.children. 3. Mark visited[0][0]=true, get next_node for 'A'. 4. Recurse to neighbor (0,1): dfs(0,1, node_A, visited). node_A has child 'C'. 5. Check: grid[0][1]='B' not in node_A.children, return false. 6. Recurse to neighbor (1,0): dfs(1,0, node_A, visited). grid[1][0]='C' in node_A.children. 7. Mark visited[1][0]=true, get next_node for 'C' (end node). 8. Recurse to neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) 'D' not in node_C.children, (2,0) out of bounds, (1,-1) out of bounds. All return false. 9. At (1,0): node_C.is_end is true, return true. 10. Back to (0,0): result=true, unmark visited[0][0]=false, return true. Wait, correction: Actually from step 6, grid[1][0]='C' matches, so it should return true. Let's re-trace: At (0,0), after checking (0,1) false, check (1,0): matches 'C', reaches end, returns true. So output should be true. Let's use a different example where it returns false: word='AD'. 1. dfs(0,0, root): 'A' matches, mark visited, node_A. 2. Neighbors: (0,1) 'B' not in node_A.children (needs 'D'), (1,0) 'C' not in node_A.children, (0,-1) out, (-1,0) out. All false. 3. Unmark visited, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie-Based Prefix Pruning in Backtracking
ladders.desc: Using a trie to stop DFS exploration when current path cannot form a valid prefix.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the optimization that makes backtracking efficient for word search. The intuition is that in standard backtracking, we explore all possible paths of length L (4^L possibilities from each cell), but many paths become invalid early because they don't match prefixes of the target word. By using a trie (prefix tree), we can check at each step if the current sequence of characters is a prefix of the target word. If not, we prune the search immediately. The algorithm involves building a trie where each node represents a character, and a path from root to a node represents a prefix. During DFS, we traverse the trie simultaneously: start at root, and for each grid cell visited, move to the child node corresponding to the cell's character. If no such child exists, the path is invalid. If we reach a node marked as end-of-word, we found the word. Time complexity improvement: without pruning, O(m*n*4^L); with trie, still O(m*n*4^L) in worst case but much better average case. Space complexity for trie is O(T) where T is total characters in words. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the DFS function where we check node.children. Connection to L3: The trie data structure enables this pruning. Common mistakes: not building trie correctly, confusing trie traversal with word index, not handling multiple words. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: children={}, is_end=False; insert(word): node=root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char]=TrieNode(); node=node.children[char]; node.is_end=True. During DFS: if char not in node.children: return false; next_node=node.children[char]; recurse with next_node.

examples.input: word='CAT', current path characters='C', next cell char='B'
examples.trace: 1. Build trie for 'CAT': root->C->A->T (end). 2. During DFS, start at root. After visiting 'C', move to node C. 3. Next cell has char 'B'. Check node C.children: contains 'A' but not 'B'. 4. Since 'B' not in children, current path 'CB' is not a prefix of 'CAT'. 5. Prune search: return false from this branch without exploring further.

examples.input: word='DOG', current path characters='D', next cell char='O'
examples.trace: 1. Build trie for 'DOG': root->D->O->G (end). 2. During DFS, start at root. After visiting 'D', move to node D. 3. Next cell has char 'O'. Check node D.children: contains 'O'. 4. Since 'O' is in children, current path 'DO' is a prefix of 'DOG'. 5. Continue search: move to node O, mark visited, recurse to neighbors.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure for Prefix Storage
ladders.desc: Implementing a trie (prefix tree) to store words and check prefixes efficiently.
ladders.explanation: A trie is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings, where keys are usually strings. It is particularly useful for prefix-based operations like checking if a given string is a prefix of any word in the set. The intuition is that instead of storing words in a list or hash set (which would require O(L) time to check prefixes by scanning all words), a trie allows prefix checking in O(L) time where L is the length of the prefix, by traversing nodes corresponding to each character. Each node in the trie contains a dictionary mapping characters to child nodes, and a boolean flag indicating if the node represents the end of a word. To insert a word, we start at the root and for each character, we create a child node if it doesn't exist, then move to that node. At the end, mark the node as end-of-word. To check if a string is a prefix, we traverse from root following characters; if we can traverse all characters, it's a prefix. To check if it's a complete word, we also verify the end flag. Time complexity for insert and prefix check is O(L) where L is word length. Space complexity is O(T) where T is total characters in all words. Connection to L2: This trie enables the prefix pruning logic. Connection to L4: The trie uses dictionary operations for children. Common mistakes: not initializing children dictionary properly, forgetting to set is_end flag, not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False; class Trie: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def insert(self, word): node = self.root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.is_end = True; def search(self, word): node = self.root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: return False; node = node.children[char]; return node.is_end; def startsWith(self, prefix): node = self.root; for char in prefix: if char not in node.children: return False; node = node.children[char]; return True.

examples.trace: 1. Start with root node. 2. Insert 'CAT': char 'C' not in root.children, create node C, move to node C. Char 'A' not in node C.children, create node A, move to node A. Char 'T' not in node A.children, create node T, move to node T. Set node T.is_end=True. 3. Insert 'CAR': char 'C' in root.children, move to node C. Char 'A' in node C.children, move to node A. Char 'R' not in node A.children, create node R, move to node R. Set node R.is_end=True. 4. Result: root->C->A->T (end) and A->R (end).

examples.input: Check prefix 'CA' in trie from previous example
examples.trace: 1. Start at root. 2. Char 'C': in root.children, move to node C. 3. Char 'A': in node C.children, move to node A. 4. All characters traversed, return true (prefix exists).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Moving through a 2D grid while checking boundaries and visited cells.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves navigating a 2D grid (matrix) by moving to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) while ensuring we don't go out of bounds and don't revisit cells in the current path. The intuition is that in word search, we need to explore contiguous paths, so we must be able to move to neighboring cells, but we must avoid invalid moves that would break the problem constraints. The algorithm uses a visited matrix (same dimensions as grid) of booleans to track cells already used in the current path. Before moving to a new cell (i,j), we check: 1. Is i within [0, m-1] and j within [0, n-1]? (bounds check) 2. Is visited[i][j] false? (not visited in current path). If both true, we can proceed. After processing the cell, we mark it visited, then later unmark it when backtracking. Time complexity for each move is O(1). Space complexity is O(m*n) for visited matrix. Connection to L1: This operation is part of the DFS function where we check neighbors. Connection to L5: This builds on array indexing and boolean logic. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds checking, not initializing visited matrix, forgetting to unmark visited. Real code pattern: directions = [(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni, nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n and not visited[ni][nj]: # proceed. visited[i][j] = true; # recurse; visited[i][j] = false.

examples.input: grid 2x2, current cell (0,0), visited all false
examples.trace: 1. Grid size m=2, n=2. Current cell (0,0). 2. Check up: (0-1,0) = (-1,0) -> i=-1 <0, invalid. 3. Check down: (0+1,0) = (1,0) -> i=1<2, j=0<2, visited[1][0]=false, valid. 4. Check left: (0,0-1) = (0,-1) -> j=-1<0, invalid. 5. Check right: (0,0+1) = (0,1) -> i=0<2, j=1<2, visited[0][1]=false, valid. 6. Valid neighbors: (1,0) and (0,1).

examples.input: grid 3x3, current cell (1,1), visited with (1,1) true, (0,1) true
examples.trace: 1. Grid size m=3, n=3. Current cell (1,1), but visited[1][1]=true, so actually we shouldn't be here if checking from a neighbor perspective. Let's assume we're at (1,1) and checking its neighbors. visited[1][1] is already true, but for neighbor check, we check visited of neighbors. 2. Check up: (0,1) -> i=0<3, j=1<3, but visited[0][1]=true, invalid. 3. Check down: (2,1) -> i=2<3, j=1<3, visited[2][1]=false, valid. 4. Check left: (1,0) -> i=1<3, j=0<3, visited[1][0]=false, valid. 5. Check right: (1,2) -> i=1<3, j=2<3, visited[1][2]=false, valid. 6. Valid neighbors: (2,1), (1,0), (1,2).

testCases.input: grid 3x3, cell (1,1), visited with all neighbors true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Boolean Logic
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in a 2D array and using boolean conditions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves basic operations on a 2D array (grid) and boolean logic, which are prerequisites for grid traversal. The intuition is that a 2D array is a collection of rows and columns, where each element can be accessed using two indices: grid[i][j] gives the element at row i and column j. In programming, indices typically start at 0. Boolean logic involves conditions that evaluate to true or false, used for checks like bounds checking (0 <= i < m) and visited status. For example, to check if a cell (i,j) is within a grid of size m rows and n columns, we verify i >= 0 AND i < m AND j >= 0 AND j < n. If any part is false, the cell is out of bounds. Similarly, a visited matrix uses boolean values to track state. Time complexity for indexing is O(1). Space complexity for a grid is O(m*n). Connection to L4: This is used in bounds checking and visited checks. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using <= instead of <), confusing row/column order, not initializing arrays. Real code pattern: m = len(grid); n = len(grid[0]); element = grid[i][j]; if i >= 0 and i < m and j >= 0 and j < n: # in bounds; visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; if not visited[i][j]: # not visited.

examples.trace: 1. Grid has 2 rows (m=2) and 2 columns (n=2). 2. Access grid[0][1]: row 0, column 1. 3. Row 0 is ['A','B']. Column 1 is the second element: 'B'. 4. So grid[0][1] = 'B'.

examples.input: grid = [['X','Y','Z']], i=0, j=2, check bounds with m=1, n=3
examples.trace: 1. m=1 (number of rows), n=3 (number of columns). 2. Check i=0: 0 >=0 true, 0 <1 true. 3. Check j=2: 2 >=0 true, 2 <3 true. 4. All conditions true, so in bounds. 5. Access grid[0][2]: row 0 is ['X','Y','Z'], column 2 is third element: 'Z'.

testCases.input: grid = [['M','N'],['O','P']], check bounds for i=1, j=1 with m=2, n=2
grid = [['M','N'],['O','P']], check bounds for i=-1, j=0
